front: Film: stars Maria Falconetti as Joan of Arc in iconic extreme close-ups with soft lighting; inquisitors filmed at low angles with harsh lighting to appear grotesque
back: The Passion of Joan of Arc (Carl Theodor Dreyer)
work: The Passion of Joan of Arc (1928)

front: Film: almost entirely shot in close-up with no establishing shots; David Bordwell found fewer than 30 of 1500 cuts maintain visual continuity
back: The Passion of Joan of Arc (Carl Theodor Dreyer)
work: The Passion of Joan of Arc (1928)

front: Film: Antoine Artaud plays a sympathetic monk; a grating casts a cross-shaped shadow; Joan wears a straw crown while crying
back: The Passion of Joan of Arc (Carl Theodor Dreyer)
work: The Passion of Joan of Arc (1928)

front: Film: based on actual 1431 trial transcripts; Pauline Kael called Falconetti's performance the greatest acting performance ever
back: The Passion of Joan of Arc (Carl Theodor Dreyer)
work: The Passion of Joan of Arc (1928)

front: Film: rediscovered in a psychiatric hospital in 1981; Richard Einhorn's 'Voices of Light' was inspired by it
back: The Passion of Joan of Arc (Carl Theodor Dreyer)
work: The Passion of Joan of Arc (1928)

front: Film: Johannes (farmer's son) believes he is Jesus Christ after going mad studying Kierkegaard; claims he can raise the dead
Film: ~3-minute 360-degree rotating camera shot around a girl and her uncle discussing her mother's afterlife/resurrection
Film: protagonist dreams of seeing his own dead body in a coffin, then a corpse-eye view of its funeral procession
Film: a shadow walks independently of the person casting it; a sinister doctor is finally suffocated by flour at a mill
Film: starred the socialite Nicolas de Gunzburg; based on Sheridan Le Fanu's Carmilla
Film: made during Nazi occupation; allegorizes it through a 17th-century witch hunt; a woman falsely confesses to killing her husband with witchcraft after reading the Song of Solomon to his son
Film: depicts the homosexual relationship between painter Claude Zoret and his model/protégé Michael; one of cinema's earliest gay-themed films
Director: Paul Schrader analyzed Dreyer (alongside Ozu and Bresson) in Transcendental Style in Film, contrasting expressionism and transcendentalism
Director: Robert Bresson criticized his Passion of Joan of Arc for 'grotesque buffooneries' and preferred his own version of Joan's trial
